text: The surgeon enters the right chest cavity, creating a pneumothorax with a pressure of eight millimeters of mercury. The patient is positioned prone, and double lung ventilation is used, resulting in the visible collapse of the lung.

The surgeon creates a plane between the pericardium and the posterior aspect of the esophagus in the prone-positioned patient. The pericardium is visible anteriorly, while the dissection occurs behind the esophagus. Direct blood vessels from the aorta supplying the esophagus are coagulated and divided using a monopolar hook.
The surgeon carefully releases the lung attachment from the inferior pulmonary ligament, visible within the right chest cavity.

The surgeon moves caudally, revealing the diaphragm and creating a plane between the right crus and the esophagus. The lower part of the esophagus is completely mobilized. Moving cranially, a clear plane is visible between the esophageal vein and the esophagus, with the tracheal bifurcation and membranous part of the right bronchus also in view.

The surgeon uses a silk suture to ligate the azygos vein, placing the first ligature on the proximal side to prevent overfilling. The ligation occurs near the junction with the superior vena cava. After applying clips, the azygos vein is divided, marking the only structure requiring division in the thoracic part of esophageal mobilization.

The surgeon dissects on the anterior aspect of the esophagus in the subcarinal region, where minimal space is observed between the esophagus and the membranous part of the trachea, requiring careful manipulation to avoid excessive diathermy application.

The surgeon extends the dissection cranially, clearing nodal tissues along the subcarinal region, which are placed in an endobag. The esophagus is fully mobilized. The patient is repositioned from prone to supine, and five ports are used for the abdominal part. The gastrocolic omentum is opened, and a plane is created between the duodenum and the transverse mesocolon.

The surgeon carefully preserves the right gastroepiploic pedicle, which supplies the gastric tube, ensuring no trauma occurs to this vessel. Concurrently, the attachments between the anterior surface of the pancreas and the posterior surface of the stomach are divided, extending up to the duodenum.

The surgeon identifies the gastro duodenal artery crossing behind the duodenum. The short gastric vessels are divided as the dissection progresses upwards, with the gastric fundus closely approaching the spleen. Careful dissection is performed along the spleen to avoid undue traction on the stomach.

The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament, also known as the lesser omentum, extending the incision cranially up to the diaphragm. The left gastric pedicle is exposed, and a lymph node is visible along the common hepatic artery, which is planned for removal later.

The surgeon divides the attachment of the esophagus to the diaphragm, revealing the inferior phrenic vessel. The left gastric vein is mobilized, and nodal tissue along the left gastric pedicle is cleared. Previously cleared nodal tissues along the upper part of the pancreas and the common hepatic artery are also visible.
The surgeon divides the left gastric artery, with the surrounding nodal tissue along the common hepatic artery visibly cleared.

The surgeon further mobilizes the hiatus, revealing the retroesophageal window. The fundus of the stomach is visible, along with the cleared nodes along the superior border of the pancreas and the course of the spleen.

The surgeon clears nodal tissues along the left gastric pedicle, common hepatic artery, and splenic artery. The dissection reveals the anatomical space between the aorta and esophagus, with the course of the aorta clearly visible.

The surgeon makes a skin crease incision in the neck and divides the esophagus. A Ryles tube is attached to the distal segment of the esophagus, which is then pulled into the abdomen through a small incision. The esophageal specimen is brought out through the abdominal incision.

The surgeon removes the esophageal specimen and creates a gastric tube using a stapler. The Riles tube is attached to the gastric tube, and as the Riles tube is pulled through the neck, the gastric tube follows and is visible in the neck area. A pyloroplasty is performed before completing the procedure.
